title: Guhai Namashivaya
meta_description: Guhai Namasivaya was a Virasaiva yogi born around AD 1548 in Karnataka who settled in a cave on Arunachala and spent 200 years as an ardent devotee of the Red Mountain Lord.
body_html:
Visitors to Virupaksha Cave, the place where Ramana Maharshi spent about fifteen years of his life, may have noticed a small walled compound a few hundred feet lower down the hill. This compound contains a cave that was, several centuries ago, occupied for many years by a yogi called Guhai Namasivaya. The cave and the few buildings that surround it now bear his name.

Guhai Namasivaya is known to have been born around the year AD 1548 in Karnataka to a pious Saiva couple. He became a disciple of Sivananda Desikar, a famous Guru who lived at Sri Sailam, and immersed himself in the Virasaiva tradition for many years.

The Virasaivas believe that the term Linga is synonymous with chaitanya, or consciousness, and can be taken to be Siva himself. The goal of Virasaivas is the attainment of oneness with Siva through a competent Guru's grace. Guhai Namasivaya became an accomplished Siva yogi under this system.

When he had thoroughly mastered Siva Yoga, Lord Mallikarjuna appeared to Guhai Namasivaya in a dream and commanded him to go to Arunachala. Shortly afterwards, Guhai Namasivaya set out for Tiruvannamalai, accompanied by Virupaksha Deva.

On reaching Tiruvannamalai, Guhai Namasivaya took a vow never to stay in any house. He devoted himself to Siva Yoga, visited the entrance of the Arunachaleswara Temple each day, and eventually received a divine command to move into a cave on the slopes of the eastern side of the hill. He spent the remainder of his life in this cave, acquiring the title 'Guhai' (Tamil for cave).

The mountain became the main focus of his sadhana. He recognised that the Virasaiva concept of Linga as a blazing column of light in the heart was identical to how Siva manifested at Arunachala. He began to worship Arunachala as his Guru Linga:

Taking into my heart as my Guru the Red Mountain Lord [Arunachala], who now stands formless before me, I have put to flight the unutterable arrogance of my good and evil deeds...

Guhai Namasivaya composed a four-line venba verse each day in praise of Arunachala. Only two of his poems have been published: Arunagiri Antadi (100 verses) and Tiruvarunai Tanivenba (36 verses). Though born in Karnataka speaking Kannada, he thoroughly mastered Tamil and his poetry is used as a text to illustrate the difficult venba metre.

When Guhai Namasivaya reached 100 years of age, he had a samadhi pit prepared, but Lord Siva ordered him to stay a further 100 years on earth. One hundred years later, after composing his final two verses, he descended into a samadhi pit in the cave bearing his name and gave up his life. His disciples erected a Linga on the spot and daily puja has been conducted there ever since.

By Nadhia Sutara — First appeared in Mountain Path
notes: No hero image before H1. Long article by Nadhia Sutara.
